items.prompt_md: What is the general shape of an exchange-argument greedy proof?
options: Assume an optimal solution differs from the greedy one at some point, then show swapping the greedy choice in never makes it worse
Try every possible ordering and pick the best one by brute force
Compute a DP table over all subsets of the input
Randomly sample orderings until one happens to work
items.answer: Assume an optimal solution differs from the greedy one at some point, then show swapping the greedy choice in never makes it worse
items.explanation_md: The exchange argument is a swap proof: any optimal solution can be transformed into the greedy one, one swap at a time, without losing optimality.

items.prompt_md: Why do most exchange-argument greedy algorithms sort the input first?
options: Sorting establishes an order where comparing 'the greedy pick' to 'any other candidate' becomes a simple local comparison instead of checking all pairs
Sorting is required by the definition of the word 'greedy'
Sorting guarantees the algorithm uses O(1) extra space
Sorting removes the need for any correctness proof at all
items.answer: Sorting establishes an order where comparing 'the greedy pick' to 'any other candidate' becomes a simple local comparison instead of checking all pairs
items.explanation_md: Once sorted, the 'next best' candidate is always adjacent in the order, so a single domination argument covers a whole batch instead of every pair individually.

items.prompt_md: If sorting dominates the runtime of an exchange-argument greedy algorithm, what is the overall time complexity for n elements?
options: O(n log n)
O(n)
O(log n)
items.answer: O(n log n)
items.explanation_md: The sort itself costs O(n log n), and the single greedy pass afterward is only O(n), so sorting sets the overall bound.

items.prompt_md: In an 'assign cookies' style problem (children each with a greed threshold, cookies each with a size, maximizing satisfied children), why does sorting BOTH lists make the greedy correct?
options: Giving the smallest sufficient cookie to the least-greedy unsatisfied child never wastes a cookie that a greedier child could have used just as well
Sorting guarantees every child receives a cookie regardless of size
Sorting converts the assignment problem into a graph search
It removes the need to compare cookie size against greed threshold at all
items.answer: Giving the smallest sufficient cookie to the least-greedy unsatisfied child never wastes a cookie that a greedier child could have used just as well
items.explanation_md: Using the smallest cookie that still satisfies the least-greedy child preserves every larger cookie for children who need more — an exchange never improves on this.

items.prompt_md: For a 'maximum non-overlapping intervals' problem, why does sorting by END time (not start time) make the exchange argument work?
options: Picking the interval that finishes earliest always leaves the most room for future intervals — any other choice's finish time is at least as late, so it can never leave more room
Sorting by end time makes the intervals disjoint automatically, with no further work needed
Start-time sorting is mathematically equivalent and correctness never depends on the choice
End-time sorting reduces the problem's time complexity to O(1)
items.answer: Picking the interval that finishes earliest always leaves the most room for future intervals — any other choice's finish time is at least as late, so it can never leave more room
items.explanation_md: The earliest-finishing interval is never a worse first pick: swapping it for any later-finishing interval can only shrink the remaining room for future picks.

items.prompt_md: In the gas-station problem, what does the exchange argument prove about stations strictly between a failed candidate start and the point of failure?
options: Each of them had a nonnegative running surplus measured from the candidate start, so starting there instead throws away that surplus and fails no later
They must all individually have negative gas values
They can be safely deleted from the array entirely
They are guaranteed to be the correct answer instead of the original candidate
items.answer: Each of them had a nonnegative running surplus measured from the candidate start, so starting there instead throws away that surplus and fails no later
items.explanation_md: A station in between only had a nonnegative running total because the tank hadn't failed yet — restarting there discards accumulated surplus rather than gaining any.

items.prompt_md: What is the time complexity of testing every station as a candidate start by simulating the full circuit from scratch each time?
options: O(n)
O(n log n)
O(log n)
items.explanation_md: Each of the n candidate starts requires an O(n) simulation of the full circuit, giving quadratic total work.

items.prompt_md: A buggy gas-station implementation resets `tank` to 0 on a negative dip but forgets to also advance `start` to i + 1. What breaks?
options: The algorithm keeps re-testing stations already proven dead, since it never actually skips the dominated block
It becomes asymptotically slower but still returns the correct answer
It causes an index-out-of-range error immediately on the next iteration
Nothing breaks — start and tank track independent, unrelated facts
items.answer: The algorithm keeps re-testing stations already proven dead, since it never actually skips the dominated block
items.explanation_md: Advancing start is what actually applies the exchange argument's conclusion — without it, the proof is known but never acted on.

items.prompt_md: What must be true across the WHOLE array before you can trust the gas-station greedy's chosen start index?
options: The total surplus (sum of gas minus cost) over the entire trip must be nonnegative; otherwise no valid start exists at all
The array must already be sorted by gas value before scanning
Every individual station must independently satisfy gas >= cost
The array length must be an even number
items.answer: The total surplus (sum of gas minus cost) over the entire trip must be nonnegative; otherwise no valid start exists at all
items.explanation_md: A candidate start surviving the scan only tells you where a circuit would begin IF one exists; total >= 0 is the separate condition that one exists.

items.prompt_md: When does a sort-then-pick exchange-argument greedy fail to be provably optimal — a trap worth watching for?
options: When the locally best choice at each step can foreclose a globally better combination later, so no swap argument can show the greedy choice never hurts
It never fails once the input has been sorted
Only when the array contains duplicate values
Only when n happens to be an odd number
items.answer: When the locally best choice at each step can foreclose a globally better combination later, so no swap argument can show the greedy choice never hurts
items.explanation_md: If you cannot construct a swap proof — showing any deviation from the greedy pick is never strictly better — the greedy-choice property doesn't hold, and the algorithm may return a suboptimal answer.

items.prompt_md: What's the key difference between this lesson's sort-then-pick exchange-argument greedy and the previous lesson's reachability greedy?
options: Here, the input's original order isn't meaningful, so sorting by some criterion is what exposes the domination relationship; reachability greedy relied on the array's existing position order instead
There is no real difference; both are exactly the same algorithm applied to different data
Exchange-argument greedy never uses a running total of any kind
Reachability greedy also always requires sorting the array first
items.answer: Here, the input's original order isn't meaningful, so sorting by some criterion is what exposes the domination relationship; reachability greedy relied on the array's existing position order instead
items.explanation_md: Reachability greedy scans the array's fixed positions in place; exchange-argument greedy imposes a new order (by end time, by size, etc.) before scanning.

items.prompt_md: What's the verbal trigger for reaching into sort-then-pick exchange-argument greedy before writing any code?
options: You must choose one ordering or threshold, and failing at one candidate provably dooms a whole dominated batch of others — by a comparable running measure, not just a guess
You need the shortest path through a weighted graph
You need to check whether brackets in a string are balanced
You need to find a pair of numbers summing to a target in a sorted array
items.answer: You must choose one ordering or threshold, and failing at one candidate provably dooms a whole dominated batch of others — by a comparable running measure, not just a guess
items.explanation_md: That phrase — provable domination of a whole batch via a running measure — is the tell for a single sorted scan with a reset-on-failure rule.

items.prompt_md: You have children each with a greed threshold and cookies each with a size. You want to maximize the number of content children, where a child is content only if given a cookie at least as large as their greed.
options: Sort both children and cookies, then greedily match the smallest sufficient cookie to the least-greedy child
Assign cookies to children in their original input order
Use a max-heap to always give the largest cookie first, regardless of fit
Backtrack through every possible assignment of cookies to children
items.answer: Sort both children and cookies, then greedily match the smallest sufficient cookie to the least-greedy child
items.explanation_md: Sorting exposes the domination relationship: using the smallest sufficient cookie on the least-greedy child never wastes a resource a greedier child needed.

items.prompt_md: You're given a list of intervals and want the maximum number of non-overlapping intervals you can keep.
options: Sort by end time, then greedily keep an interval if it doesn't overlap the last one kept
Sort by start time only and keep the first half of the list
Use a stack to track which intervals overlap
Two pointers moving inward from both ends of the interval list
items.answer: Sort by end time, then greedily keep an interval if it doesn't overlap the last one kept
items.explanation_md: Sorting by finish time and always keeping the earliest-finishing compatible interval is provably optimal — any swap to a later-finishing interval only shrinks future room.

items.prompt_md: You're at gas stations arranged in a circle, each with a gas amount and a cost to reach the next station. Determine the single starting station, if one exists, from which you can complete the full circuit.
options: Scan once with a running tank and total surplus, resetting the candidate start whenever the tank goes negative
Simulate the full circuit from every station independently
Use a min-heap of remaining gas at each station
Sort stations by gas amount, descending, and start at the top
items.answer: Scan once with a running tank and total surplus, resetting the candidate start whenever the tank goes negative
items.explanation_md: A failed candidate start proves every station up to the failure point is dominated and can't work either, so one reset-on-negative pass replaces a full retest per station.

items.prompt_md: You need to satisfy the maximum number of greed thresholds using the fewest sufficient cookie sizes, where both the child list and cookie list can be freely reordered.
options: Sort both lists, then greedily match the smallest sufficient cookie to each least-greedy child in turn
Assign cookies in the order they were originally listed
Use a min-heap keyed by greed threshold only, ignoring cookie size
Try every permutation of cookie-to-child assignments
items.answer: Sort both lists, then greedily match the smallest sufficient cookie to each least-greedy child in turn
items.explanation_md: This is the same cookie-assignment exchange argument: the smallest cookie that satisfies a child is never worse than reserving a bigger one for them.

items.prompt_md: Given intervals representing job durations with deadlines, you want to select the maximum number of jobs that can be completed without any two overlapping.
options: Sort by end time, then greedily accept a job if it doesn't conflict with the last accepted one
Sort by job duration, longest first
Use a stack to resolve overlapping jobs
Two pointers scanning from both ends of the sorted job list
items.answer: Sort by end time, then greedily accept a job if it doesn't conflict with the last accepted one
items.explanation_md: This is the interval-scheduling exchange argument again: the earliest-finishing job never leaves less room for future picks than any alternative would.

items.prompt_md: You want to prove that greedily assigning the smallest sufficient cookie to the least-greedy child is at least as good as any other assignment, without checking every possible pairing.
options: Construct an exchange argument: show that swapping the greedy pick for any other candidate's cookie never improves the count of satisfied children
Enumerate every pairing and compare satisfied-child counts directly
Build a DP table over all subsets of children and cookies
Use a heap to always assign the largest available cookie
items.answer: Construct an exchange argument: show that swapping the greedy pick for any other candidate's cookie never improves the count of satisfied children
items.explanation_md: This is exactly the exchange-argument proof technique: assume some optimal solution differs from greedy, then show a swap back to the greedy choice never makes it worse.

items.prompt_md: An array of (gas, cost) pairs describes a circular route. You want to avoid re-simulating the whole trip from every possible starting station.
options: Track a running tank and total surplus in one pass, jumping the candidate start forward whenever the tank goes negative
Sort the stations by cost, ascending, then simulate from the cheapest
Use a max-heap of gas surplus per station
Two pointers converging from both ends of the circular array
items.answer: Track a running tank and total surplus in one pass, jumping the candidate start forward whenever the tank goes negative
items.explanation_md: One O(n) pass replaces O(n^2) resimulation precisely because a failed candidate proves an entire dominated block also fails.

items.prompt_md: You're given a set of intervals and asked for the minimum number to REMOVE so that none of the remaining intervals overlap.
options: Sort by end time and greedily keep the maximum number of non-overlapping intervals; the rest must be removed
Sort by start time and remove every other interval
Use a stack to mark which intervals conflict
Two pointers scanning from both ends of the sorted interval list
items.answer: Sort by end time and greedily keep the maximum number of non-overlapping intervals; the rest must be removed
items.explanation_md: Minimizing removals is the same problem as maximizing the kept non-overlapping set — the end-time-sorted exchange argument still applies.

items.prompt_md: You have a list of meeting intervals and must determine the minimum number of meeting rooms needed to host all of them, where several meetings can be simultaneously active at once.
options: Sort by start time, then use a min-heap of end times to track how many meetings are simultaneously active
Sort by end time and greedily keep only non-overlapping intervals
Scan once tracking a single running surplus, like in the gas-station problem
Sort by greed threshold and assign the smallest sufficient meeting slot
items.answer: Sort by start time, then use a min-heap of end times to track how many meetings are simultaneously active
items.explanation_md: Counting simultaneously active intervals needs a count that rises and falls — a heap of active end times — not the single keep/discard decision of the non-overlapping-interval greedy, which only ever keeps or drops.

items.prompt_md: You have two sorted arrays of integers and need to find a pair, one element from each array, whose sum is as close as possible to a target value.
options: Use two pointers, one at each array's start, moving based on how the current sum compares to the target
Sort then greedily assign smallest-to-smallest, like the cookie-assignment problem
Scan once with a running surplus, like the gas-station problem
Use a stack to pair values from the two arrays
items.answer: Use two pointers, one at each array's start, moving based on how the current sum compares to the target
items.explanation_md: This is a closest-sum search across two sorted sequences, not a one-resource-per-one-consumer assignment — the cookie-style exchange argument doesn't establish optimality here; converging pointers do.

items.prompt_md: You're given an array of daily temperatures and, for each day, must find how many days until a warmer temperature occurs.
options: Maintain a stack of unresolved day indices, popping whenever a warmer day is found
Sort the temperatures, then greedily match smallest to smallest like the cookie problem
Scan once with a running surplus, like the gas-station problem
Use a min-heap keyed by temperature value
items.answer: Maintain a stack of unresolved day indices, popping whenever a warmer day is found
items.explanation_md: "Next warmer day" needs a stack of pending indices resolved in last-in-first-out order; sorting here would destroy the day-order the answer depends on.
